feedback.text: As a strong propronent of words holding meaning; I would like to suggest to have Videogame sales be included within the Digital Fairness Act. The problem: Consumers are being sold games; with no terms being clearly defined for how long said game will be active. Most games (especially older titles) are inherently able to operate for-ever. As of recent Publishers have gotten into the trend to sell games with effectively built in kill switches. A personal example is the Game "Evolve". The title was released by 2K. It had a bunch of issues initially, but it managed to get on. I purchased the game full price (at the time 60 euro). The game ended up having a massive revamp and becomming free to play. The compensation to players who bought the game was that they would have almost all unlockable characters unlocked and a set of exclusive in-game items. This was behavior I always deemed reasonable. I could still play the game I paid for. However later 2K decided to take down all the servers the game was depending on. This made the game unplayable. Variouse grass-roots attempts were made by the community to revive the game, some having more success then others. But eventually everything was snuffed out and as of today the only way to play the game is the tutorial the game shipped with. If a purchase has made the game my property, then the publisher should not be able to then later destroy what I have purchased. Sure they took down their server, but they full well knew that the game depends on it. They should have instead provided the community with means to support the game (a patch so we can point the game to a community hosted server for example). The destruction of Evolve was done with complete disregard to the product which was sold and paid for. If 2K (The publisher) was aware they planned to destory the game. Then I would've liked instead that the Store page would clearly cite a date on which the game would be destroyed(and be called a rental), or upon destruction be required to provide all purchasers a full refund. How can this case/precident fit neatly into this act? Well, I would say this fits under the problem of digital fairness for consumers being lacking. We cannot make a proper finnancial descision when purchasing a game if we do not know the terms of the deal. Now the terms could change; that is fine, but the terms should never worsen for the consumer. Under the objectives, this objective could be included in the "addressing problems with digital contracts" as ToS's publishers provide are often not in agreement with European or member-state local laws already (far too draconian and anti-consumer). Alternatively, it could be added as a new objective. Purchasing a game should not be purchasing a License to play the game. It should be purchasing the game AND a license to play the online components through the official publishers servers. Whatever happens locally on the users computer should be fine, regardless of what they do with it. The remote components that our outside of that users machine, there publishers have a stronger argument. However I do fear a loophole being created where the game is merely an entry point to a streaming application (where all game data gets streamed to the user either assets or frames) and thus publishers could continue what they do today, just need to invest into a streaming solution that actually works. Either way I fully support the act as is. Protecting Youth from influencers and dark-patterns is amazing. Happy to see something being done about it. I will always be somewhat concerned in regards to maintaining privacy (UK's recent law makes privacy almost impossible online) But none of these should impact privacy of consumers.
